cards.back: Pointer arithmetic is the ability to add or subtract integer values from pointers to navigate through memory, particularly useful for traversing arrays.

cards.text: When you add 1 to a pointer, it advances by {{c1::sizeof(type)}} bytes, not 1 byte

cards.question: If ptr points to arr[0] (an int array), what does ptr + 3 point to?
cards.explanation: ptr + 3 advances by 3 * sizeof(int) bytes, pointing to the element at index 3.

cards.text: The expression arr[n] is equivalent to {{c1::*(arr + n)}} using pointer arithmetic

cards.front: What arithmetic operations are valid on pointers?
cards.back: Addition and subtraction with integers (ptr + n, ptr - n), increment/decrement (++ptr, --ptr), and subtraction of two pointers (ptr1 - ptr2). Multiplication and division are not allowed.

cards.text: Subtracting two pointers (ptr1 - ptr2) gives the {{c1::number of elements}} between them, not the byte distance

cards.question: What does this expression access?
cards.explanation: ptr + 2 points to arr[2], and dereferencing with * gives the value 30.

cards.front: What happens if you use pointer arithmetic to go beyond array bounds?
cards.back: It causes undefined behavior. The pointer may point to invalid memory, and dereferencing it can crash the program or corrupt data.

cards.text: The subscript operator [] works on {{c1::any pointer}}, not just arrays: ptr[n] is valid for any pointer ptr

cards.explanation:
Pointer arithmetic use cases:
  • Low-level memory manipulation
  • Implementing custom data structures
  • Performance-critical code with arrays
Avoid when:
  • Standard containers work fine (std::vector, std::array)
  • Readability matters more than micro-optimization
  • Working with modern C++ code
examples: for (int* p = arr; p < arr + size; ++p)  // Pointer iteration
for (auto& elem : arr)  // Prefer this in modern C++

cards.text: When incrementing a pointer with ++ptr, it moves to the {{c1::next element}}, not the next byte

cards.question: Are these two loops equivalent?
cards.answer: Yes, they both print the same values
cards.explanation: Loop 1 uses array indexing, Loop 2 uses pointer arithmetic. Both access the same elements in the same order.

cards.front: Is pointer arithmetic valid on nullptr?
cards.back: No, pointer arithmetic on nullptr is undefined behavior. The pointer must point to valid memory (or one past the end of an array).
